walkthrough.title: Choose a Scenario
walkthrough.text: Let's get Gloomhaven set up. First, open the scenario book and pick a scenario. If this is your first campaign, we are starting with Scenario one, Black Barrow. Read the introduction text aloud to the group so everyone knows the story context and what we are trying to accomplish.

walkthrough.title: Build the Starting Room
walkthrough.text: Now find the map tiles listed in the scenario diagram. We only set up the first room right now. Do not reveal any rooms beyond the first closed door. Lay out the tiles exactly as shown in the scenario book, matching the shape and orientation carefully.

walkthrough.title: Place Overlay Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's add the overlay tiles to the starting room. Place door overlays between rooms as indicated, trap overlays on marked hexes, obstacle overlays like rocks and bookcases on their hexes, and any difficult or hazardous terrain. Finally, place any numbered treasure tiles where the scenario diagram shows them.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Room Monsters
walkthrough.text: Now we place the monsters for the first room only. Check the scenario diagram for which monster types go where. Normal monsters get white plastic bases and elites get gold bases. The number of monsters placed depends on your party size, which is shown on the scenario page.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Monster Stats and Decks
walkthrough.text: For each monster type in the scenario, find their stat card and slide it into a stat sleeve. Set the HP dial to match the scenario level. Then find each monster type's ability card deck and give it a good shuffle. Place these decks face down near the map.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Element Board and Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the element board near the map with all six element tokens set to Inert at the bottom. Set out the shared supply of damage tokens, condition tokens, and money tokens where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Character Class
walkthrough.text: Now each player picks a character class. For a new campaign, six classes are available: Brute, Tinkerer, Spellweaver, Scoundrel, Cragheart, and Mindthief. Take your class tuck box and pull out the miniature, ability cards, character sheet pad, and attack modifier cards. Fill out a fresh character sheet with your character name and level one stats.

walkthrough.title: Draw Personal Quest and Select Ability Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player draws two personal quest cards and keeps one in secret. This is your character's retirement goal. Then take all your level one and level X ability cards and choose exactly your hand size number of cards to bring into the scenario. Your hand size is printed on your character sheet.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Your Attack Modifier Deck
walkthrough.text: Take the standard twenty-card attack modifier deck. It contains six plus-zero cards, five plus-one cards, five minus-one cards, one plus-two, one minus-two, one times-zero Miss, and one times-two Critical. If your character has earned any perks, modify the deck accordingly. Shuffle it and place it face down.

walkthrough.title: Buy Items and Equip
walkthrough.text: New level one characters start with thirty gold. Visit the Gloomhaven shop and buy items from the available supply. Each character can equip one head item, one body item, one legs item, up to two one-hand items or one two-hand item, and a number of small items equal to half your level rounded up. Place your item cards face up near your character sheet.

walkthrough.title: Set HP and Draw Battle Goal
walkthrough.text: Set your HP dial to your maximum hit points for level one. Then each player draws two battle goal cards and secretly keeps one. Completing your battle goal during the scenario earns you a perk checkmark, which permanently improves your modifier deck.

walkthrough.title: Resolve Road Event
walkthrough.text: If the scenario is not in Gloomhaven or directly linked from your previous location, draw the top card of the Road Event deck. Read the narrative aloud, collectively choose option A or option B, then flip the card and apply the consequences. Effects might include gaining or losing gold, HP, conditions, or reputation.

walkthrough.title: Place Characters and Verify
walkthrough.text: Place your character miniatures in the designated starting hexes shown in the scenario diagram. If multiple starting hexes are available, decide collectively who starts where because positioning matters for your opening tactics.

walkthrough.title: Final Checks and Begin
walkthrough.text: Let's do our final checks. Make sure all monster ability decks and all attack modifier decks are shuffled. Re-read any special scenario rules and win conditions. Confirm everyone's HP is set to max. We are ready to begin round one. All players simultaneously select their first two ability cards.

summary.title: Final Checks and Begin

Verify HP, re-read special rules → begin round 1
All players simultaneously select 2 ability cards

walkthrough.title: Round Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how a round works in Gloomhaven. At the start of every round, all players simultaneously and secretly choose two ability cards from their hand. You will play the top half of one card and the bottom half of the other. Instead of choosing two cards, you may declare a long rest, which we will cover later.

walkthrough.title: Declare Initiative
walkthrough.text: After selecting your two cards, each player announces the initiative number printed on one of their chosen cards. This number determines when you act this round. Lower numbers go first. Monster initiative is revealed by flipping the top card of each active monster type's ability deck.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Let's sort everyone from lowest initiative to highest. That is our turn order for this round. If two players tie, they decide between themselves who goes first. If a player ties with a monster group, the player decides who goes first. Each entity takes its full turn before moving to the next.

walkthrough.title: Playing Your Two Cards
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you play the top half of one selected card and the bottom half of the other, in whichever order you choose. Fully resolve one half-action before starting the other. Instead of using a card's printed action, you may always use a default Attack two for any top half or a default Move two for any bottom half.

walkthrough.title: Discarding and Losing Cards
walkthrough.text: After both half-actions are resolved, place both cards in your discard pile. However, if either action has the loss icon, that card goes to your lost pile instead. Lost cards cannot be recovered for the rest of the scenario. This is critical because your hand of cards is essentially your life timer.

walkthrough.title: Move Actions
walkthrough.text: Let's cover the basic action types. A Move action lets you move up to the listed number of hexes. You cannot enter hexes occupied by enemies, but you can move through allies without stopping on them. Obstacles block movement entirely. Difficult terrain costs two movement points to enter, and traps trigger when you enter their hex.

walkthrough.title: Attack Actions
walkthrough.text: An Attack action lets you deal damage to an enemy. Melee attacks target adjacent hexes. Ranged attacks can hit enemies within the listed range, but attacking at range while adjacent to any enemy gives you Disadvantage. For every single attack, you draw one card from your attack modifier deck to adjust the damage.

walkthrough.title: Attack Modifier Cards
walkthrough.text: Your modifier deck adjusts every attack. You might draw a plus-one, a minus-one, a times-zero Miss that deals no damage, or a times-two Critical that doubles your base attack. When the Miss or Critical card is drawn, the deck reshuffles at the end of the round. Perks let you permanently improve this deck over time.

walkthrough.title: Advantage and Disadvantage
walkthrough.text: Some effects grant Advantage or Disadvantage on attacks. With Advantage, draw two modifier cards and use the better one. With Disadvantage, draw two and use the worse one. Strengthen gives Advantage on all attacks next turn, while Muddle gives Disadvantage on all attacks next turn.

walkthrough.title: Conditions
walkthrough.text: Conditions add lasting effects to characters and monsters. Poison makes all incoming damage deal plus-one. Wound deals one damage at the start of each of your turns. Stun causes you to lose your entire next turn. Immobilize prevents all movement. Disarm prevents all attacks. Healing removes Poison or Wound first, consuming the heal without restoring HP.

walkthrough.title: Healing, Shield, and Retaliate
walkthrough.text: A Heal action restores hit points up to your maximum, but remember it removes Poison or Wound first. Shield reduces each incoming attack's damage by the listed amount. Retaliate deals automatic damage back to any enemy that attacks you from within the specified range. Retaliate does not use attack modifiers.

walkthrough.title: Elements
walkthrough.text: Some ability cards generate elements when used. There are six elements: Fire, Ice, Air, Earth, Light, and Dark. When you generate an element, move its token to Strong on the element board at the end of your turn. Other actions can consume an element for a bonus effect. You cannot generate and consume the same element on the same turn.

walkthrough.title: Element Waning Cycle
walkthrough.text: At the end of each round, Strong elements move to Waning and Waning elements move to Inert. This means you have a narrow window to consume an element after it is generated. Elements are shared among all figures, so monsters can generate and consume them too. Plan your element consumption one round ahead.

walkthrough.title: Looting
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about how you collect gold. A Loot action lets you pick up all money tokens and treasure tiles within the listed range. Loot one means your hex and all adjacent hexes. Money tokens translate directly to gold. Numbered treasure tiles grant rewards listed in the scenario book. Gold is your main source of income for buying items between scenarios.

walkthrough.title: Monster Turns
walkthrough.text: All monsters of the same type share one ability card flipped at the start of the round. Elites of a type act before normals. Each monster individually determines its focus by finding the enemy it can reach and attack with the least movement. Monsters move the minimum hexes needed to attack their focus and will not move if they can already attack.

walkthrough.title: Short Rest
walkthrough.text: At the end of any round, you may take a short rest. Pick up all cards from your discard pile, then randomly lose one card to your lost pile. The remaining cards return to your hand. If you do not like the randomly lost card, you may suffer one damage to randomly lose a different card instead. A short rest does not cost you a turn.

walkthrough.title: Long Rest
walkthrough.text: Instead of selecting two cards at the start of a round, you may declare a long rest. Your initiative is automatically ninety-nine, so you go last. On your turn, pick up all discards, choose one card to lose, heal two HP, and refresh all spent items. You do not play any ability cards on a long rest turn.

walkthrough.title: Exhaustion
walkthrough.text: If at the start of a round you have fewer than two cards in your hand and cannot rest, you are exhausted and eliminated from the scenario. You also take damage to cards instead of HP. When you take damage, you may negate it by losing one card from your hand or two from your discard. If all characters are exhausted, the scenario is failed.

walkthrough.title: Opening Doors and Revealing Rooms
walkthrough.text: When any figure enters a closed door hex, the door opens immediately and the current turn pauses. Set up the newly revealed room with all its map tiles, overlays, monsters, and treasure as shown in the scenario book. New monsters act on their type's already-drawn initiative card. Then the figure that opened the door continues their remaining actions.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Cleanup
walkthrough.text: After everyone has taken their turn, we clean up. Strong elements move to Waning and Waning elements move to Inert. If any modifier deck or monster ability deck triggered a shuffle icon this round, shuffle it now. Check whether the scenario's victory or defeat conditions have been met.

walkthrough.title: Scenario Victory
walkthrough.text: Each scenario has a specific objective, usually defeating all enemies in the final room. When the objective is fulfilled, the scenario ends immediately. All remaining monsters are defeated and all money tokens on the map are looted by the closest character. Players keep all XP and gold earned, plus the scenario's listed completion rewards.

walkthrough.title: Scenario Failure
walkthrough.text: If all characters are exhausted before the objective is complete, the scenario is failed. You keep all XP and gold earned during the attempt but receive no scenario completion rewards. The good news is there is no lasting penalty. You can replay the scenario immediately or adjust the difficulty before trying again.

walkthrough.title: Leveling Up Between Scenarios
walkthrough.text: Between scenarios, check if your accumulated XP reaches the next level threshold. Level two requires forty-five XP, level three requires ninety-five, and so on up to level nine at five hundred. When you level up, choose one of two new ability cards for that level, gain increased max HP, a perk checkmark, and bonus gold.

walkthrough.title: City Events and Shopping
walkthrough.text: When returning to Gloomhaven between scenarios, draw a City Event card and resolve it just like a road event. Then you can visit the shop to buy or sell items. Available items expand as Gloomhaven's prosperity level increases. You can also donate ten gold to the sanctuary for a blessing.

walkthrough.title: Retirement and Unlocking
walkthrough.text: Each character has a personal quest that defines their retirement condition. When you fulfill it, your character retires and you unlock a new sealed character class. Retirement also increases Gloomhaven's prosperity. You then create a new character of any available class at a level equal to the current prosperity level.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Black Barrow
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first scenario of Gloomhaven. We are going to walk through several rounds of Scenario one, the Black Barrow, step by step. We will use a party of three characters: a Brute, a Spellweaver, and a Scoundrel. Follow along even if your party is different. The mechanics work the same for every class.

walkthrough.title: Review the Starting Room
walkthrough.text: We have set up the first room with its monsters, a couple of Bandit Guards and a Bandit Archer. There is a closed door at the far end leading to the next room. Our three characters are placed on the starting hexes. Everyone has their hand of ability cards, their modifier deck, and their items ready to go.

walkthrough.title: Selecting Your First Two Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's start round one. Everyone secretly picks two ability cards from their hand. The Brute picks Trample and Eye for an Eye. The Spellweaver picks Frost Armor and Fire Orbs. The Scoundrel picks Quick Hands and Flanking Strike. Think about which initiative number you want to announce and what you want to accomplish this turn.

walkthrough.title: Declaring Initiative
walkthrough.text: Now each player announces an initiative number from one of their two selected cards. The Scoundrel announces initiative twelve from Quick Hands because rogues want to strike fast. The Brute announces initiative seventy-two from Eye for an Eye. The Spellweaver announces initiative twenty-six from Fire Orbs. We flip the Bandit Guard ability card and get initiative thirty.

walkthrough.title: Resolving Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Let's sort the turn order from lowest to highest initiative. Scoundrel goes first at twelve, then Spellweaver at twenty-six, then Bandit Guards at thirty, then Brute at seventy-two. Notice how the Scoundrel deliberately went fast to act before the enemies, while the Brute chose to go slow, which can be a strategic choice.

walkthrough.title: Scoundrel's Turn
walkthrough.text: The Scoundrel goes first. She uses the bottom half of Quick Hands for a Move three to get adjacent to a Bandit Guard. Then she uses the top half of Flanking Strike for an Attack three. Since the Brute is also adjacent to that Bandit Guard, the Flanking Strike bonus triggers for extra damage. She draws a plus-one modifier, dealing four total damage. Nice hit.

walkthrough.title: Spellweaver's Turn
walkthrough.text: The Spellweaver goes next. She uses the bottom half of Frost Armor to gain Shield one for the round, which will reduce incoming attacks. Then she uses the top half of Fire Orbs for a ranged Attack three against the Bandit Archer. She draws a plus-zero modifier, dealing three damage. The Fire Orbs action also generates the Fire element, so we move the Fire token to Strong.

walkthrough.title: Monster Turn
walkthrough.text: Now the Bandit Guards act. The ability card tells them to Move two and Attack two. Each guard individually focuses on the enemy it can reach with the least movement. The first guard moves adjacent to the Scoundrel and attacks. We draw from the monster modifier deck and get a plus-zero, dealing two damage. The Scoundrel takes the hit.

walkthrough.title: Brute's Turn
walkthrough.text: Finally the Brute acts at initiative seventy-two. He uses the bottom half of Trample for a Move four, pushing through a Bandit Guard's hex and dealing damage along the way. Then he uses the top half of Eye for an Eye for a Retaliate two, meaning any enemy that attacks him will take two damage back. Going slow let the Brute react to where enemies ended up.

walkthrough.title: End of Round One
walkthrough.text: Round one is done. Let's clean up. Move any Strong elements to Waning and any Waning to Inert. Fire was Strong, so it moves to Waning. Check if any modifier deck needs reshuffling. No Miss or Crit was drawn, so we are fine. All played ability cards go to discard piles unless they had a loss icon.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Card Selection Again
walkthrough.text: Let's start round two. Everyone picks two new cards from their hand. The Spellweaver has an ability that consumes Fire for a bonus effect. Since Fire is currently Waning and will become Inert at end of round, this is her last chance to consume it. She plans her cards to take advantage of the Fire element this turn.

walkthrough.title: Consuming an Element
walkthrough.text: On the Spellweaver's turn, she plays an ability that shows a Fire consume icon with an enhanced effect. She moves the Fire token from Waning to Inert and gains the bonus. This is how element timing works. You generate an element, it becomes Strong, then Waning, and you must consume it before it goes Inert. Planning one round ahead is essential.

walkthrough.title: Clearing the First Room
walkthrough.text: After a few more exchanges, the party defeats all enemies in the starting room. Enemies drop money tokens when they die. Do not forget to loot them. The coins are just sitting on the map, and they are worth gold you will need for items later. Now it is time to open that door to the next room.

walkthrough.title: Opening the Door
walkthrough.text: The Brute moves onto the closed door hex, which immediately opens it. We pause his turn and set up the new room. Place the new map tiles, overlay tokens, and monster standees exactly as shown in the scenario book. There are new Bandit Guards and a Living Bones skeleton in here. After the room is revealed, the Brute continues his remaining actions.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Burning Loss Cards Too Early
walkthrough.text: Let's pause for a common beginner mistake. The Spellweaver is tempted to play Reviving Ether, a powerful loss card, right now in room two. But this is only the second room. Loss cards cannot be recovered, and each one shortens how many rounds you can survive. If she plays it now, she may exhaust before the final room. Save powerful loss cards for when they truly matter.

walkthrough.title: Why Card Conservation Matters
walkthrough.text: Let's do some math. The Spellweaver has a hand size of eight. That means she can play roughly eight rounds before needing to rest. Each rest loses one more card from the game. Every loss card played accelerates this clock. If she burns two loss cards in room two, she might have only four or five rounds left for the rest of the scenario. That is often not enough.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Forgetting to Loot Gold
walkthrough.text: Here is another mistake new players make constantly. The Scoundrel just killed a Bandit Guard and a money token dropped on the hex. The party rushes forward to attack the next enemy and nobody picks up the coin. In Gloomhaven, you do not automatically collect gold. You must actively use a Loot action or end your movement on the coin's hex. Unlooted coins are gone when the scenario ends.

walkthrough.title: Taking a Short Rest
walkthrough.text: The Brute's hand is running low. At the end of this round, he takes a short rest. He picks up his entire discard pile, then randomly loses one card to the lost pile. He does not like which card was lost, so he suffers one damage to re-draw and loses a different card instead. The remaining cards return to his hand, and he is ready for the next round.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Poor Initiative Coordination
walkthrough.text: Round five. The Scoundrel picks a fast initiative to rush in and attack, but the Brute picks a slow initiative because he wants to use a defensive card. The problem is that the Scoundrel's Flanking Strike needs an ally adjacent to the target for bonus damage. If the Brute goes after the Scoundrel, he will not be in position yet. Always discuss initiative timing with your party before committing your cards.

walkthrough.title: Coordinating for Combos
walkthrough.text: Let's redo that. The Brute announces he will go fast this round to get into position first. The Scoundrel announces she will go right after him. Now the Brute moves adjacent to the target, and when the Scoundrel acts she has an adjacent ally for her Flanking Strike bonus. Communication about initiative is one of the most important skills in Gloomhaven.

walkthrough.title: Approaching the Final Room
walkthrough.text: The party clears room two and approaches the final door. This is where the scenario gets tense. Before opening the door, everyone checks their remaining hand cards and HP. The Brute has seven cards, the Scoundrel has five, and the Spellweaver has six. Everyone heals up with available abilities and prepares their best cards for the final fight.

walkthrough.title: The Final Room
walkthrough.text: The door opens and we reveal the final room. There is a boss enemy with high HP and several supporting monsters. Now is the time to use those loss cards we have been saving. The Spellweaver unleashes her most powerful loss action, dealing massive area damage. The Brute uses a strong loss card to tank multiple enemies. These loss cards are worth it now because the scenario is almost over.

walkthrough.title: Scenario Complete
walkthrough.text: With careful card management and coordinated attacks, the party defeats the final enemies and completes the objective. All remaining money tokens on the map are automatically looted. Each player keeps their earned XP and gold, plus the scenario's listed completion rewards. We unlock the next scenarios in the campaign and head back to Gloomhaven.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing your first Gloomhaven scenario. You learned how to select cards and manage initiative, how to time element generation and consumption, and why conserving loss cards is essential for survival. You saw three beginner mistakes that trip up new players: burning loss cards too early, forgetting to loot gold, and poor initiative coordination.

walkthrough.title: Before You Venture Forth
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into the next scenario, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy fundamentals like hand management, focus fire, and perk optimization.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all conditions, elements, and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Good luck out there.

Spellweaver plans to consume Fire (currently Waning) this turn

summary.title: Consuming an Element
bullets: Spellweaver plays card with Fire consume → Waning to Inert
Must consume before element goes Inert at end of round
Plan element usage 1 round ahead

summary.title: Clearing the First Room
bullets: Defeat all enemies in room 1 → money tokens drop
Loot coins before moving on — they won't auto-collect

summary.title: Opening the Door
bullets: Brute enters door hex → door opens, turn pauses
Set up new room per scenario book → then Brute resumes

summary.title: Beginner Mistake: Burning Loss Cards Too Early
bullets: Spellweaver tempted to play powerful loss card in room 2
Loss cards can't be recovered — each one shortens your clock
Save big losses for the final room when they matter most

summary.title: Why Card Conservation Matters
bullets: Spellweaver hand size = 8 → ~8 rounds before resting
Each rest + each loss card shrinks total available rounds
2 early losses could leave only 4-5 rounds for remaining rooms

summary.title: Beginner Mistake: Forgetting to Loot Gold
bullets: Gold does NOT auto-collect — must Loot or end movement on coin hex
Unlooted coins vanish at scenario end
Gold = items between scenarios; don't leave it on the ground

summary.title: Taking a Short Rest
bullets: Brute short rests: picks up all discards → randomly loses 1
Option: suffer 1 dmg to re-draw a different random loss
Short rest is free — no turn cost

summary.title: Beginner Mistake: Poor Initiative Coordination
bullets: Scoundrel goes fast, Brute goes slow → Flanking Strike can't trigger
Combo needs ally adjacent BEFORE Scoundrel acts
Always discuss initiative with party before selecting cards

summary.title: Coordinating for Combos
bullets: Brute goes fast to position first → Scoundrel follows for flanking bonus
Initiative coordination is a core Gloomhaven skill
Communicate timing every round

summary.title: Approaching the Final Room
bullets: Check remaining cards and HP before opening last door
Heal up, prepare best abilities for final fight

summary.title: The Final Room
bullets: Boss + supporting monsters — now use those saved loss cards
Spellweaver: AoE loss action; Brute: tanking loss card
Loss cards are worth it when scenario is nearly over

summary.title: Scenario Complete
bullets: Objective met → remaining coins auto-looted
Keep XP + gold + scenario rewards; unlock next scenarios

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Learned card selection, initiative timing, element consumption
3 beginner mistakes: burning losses early, forgetting loot, bad initiative coordination

summary.title: Before You Venture Forth
bullets: Check out General Tips → hand management, focus fire, perks
Reference the Appendix → conditions, elements, key terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Hand Management Is Everything
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Gloomhaven is managing your hand of cards. Your hand size is your life clock. Every card you lose permanently shortens how many rounds you can survive. Before playing a loss card, ask yourself: will this card do more good now than having one extra round of actions at the end of the scenario? In most cases, the extra round is worth more.

walkthrough.title: Count Your Rounds
walkthrough.text: Calculate roughly how many rounds you can last by dividing your hand size by two, rounding down, then accounting for rests. For example, a Brute with ten cards can play five rounds, rest to recover nine cards minus one lost, play four more rounds, rest again, and so on. Getting a feel for your endurance helps you decide when to play loss cards and when to rest.

walkthrough.title: Focus Fire
walkthrough.text: Concentrate your attacks on one enemy at a time rather than spreading damage across multiple targets. A monster at one HP deals the same damage as a monster at full HP. Killing an enemy removes its future attacks entirely, which means less incoming damage for your party. Coordinate with your team to pick one target and eliminate it before moving on.

walkthrough.title: Initiative Coordination
walkthrough.text: Talk with your party every single round about who needs to go fast and who should go slow. Going before enemies lets you deal damage before they can attack, while going after allies sets up combos like flanking bonuses or element consumption. Sloppy initiative wastes turns and lets enemies punish you for being out of position.

walkthrough.title: Loot Intentionally
walkthrough.text: Gold does not collect itself. You must actively loot money tokens by using a Loot action or ending your movement on a coin's hex. Plan to pick up coins when enemies drop them. Gold is your primary income for buying items between scenarios, and good items make a massive difference in your character's effectiveness. Do not leave gold sitting on the board.

walkthrough.title: Use Chokepoints and Positioning
walkthrough.text: Avoid standing adjacent to multiple enemies whenever possible. Instead, use doorways, obstacles, and narrow corridors as chokepoints. If only one enemy can attack your tank at a time, the rest of the party takes less damage. Positioning is especially important when opening a new door because the next room often has several enemies that will focus on whoever opened it.

walkthrough.title: Invest in Your Modifier Deck
walkthrough.text: Perk checkmarks permanently improve your attack modifier deck by removing bad cards and adding powerful ones. Earn perks by completing battle goals and leveling up. Prioritize removing minus-one cards first, then add rolling modifiers that stack extra damage and effects. A well-optimized modifier deck turns average attacks into devastating ones.

walkthrough.title: Buy Essential Items Early
walkthrough.text: Spend your starting gold on items that make a real difference. A Stamina Potion lets you recover two discarded cards, effectively giving you extra rounds of play. Minor Healing Potion restores five HP at a critical moment. Boots of Striding add two movement, helping you reach enemies or escape danger. Do not hoard gold. Spending it on items immediately makes you stronger for every future scenario.

summary.title: Hand Management Is Everything
bullets: Hand size = your life clock; each lost card shortens it
Ask: is this loss card worth more than 1 extra round of actions?
Usually the extra round matters more than the big loss action

summary.title: Count Your Rounds
bullets: Hand size / 2 (round down) = rounds before first rest
Account for rests (each rest loses 1 more card)
Know your endurance to plan loss cards and rest timing

summary.title: Focus Fire
bullets: Kill 1 enemy > spread damage across 3
Dead enemies deal 0 future damage
Coordinate with party to pick targets together

summary.title: Initiative Coordination
bullets: Discuss fast vs. slow every round with your party
Fast = act before enemies; slow = set up combos
Poor initiative → wasted turns, missed synergies

summary.title: Loot Intentionally
bullets: Gold does NOT auto-collect; Loot action or end movement on coin hex
Gold → items between scenarios; items = power
Don't leave coins on the board

summary.title: Use Chokepoints and Positioning
bullets: Avoid standing adjacent to multiple enemies
Doorways, obstacles, corridors = natural chokepoints
Be careful who opens doors — next room focuses on them

summary.title: Invest in Your Modifier Deck
bullets: Perks from battle goals + leveling up → permanent deck upgrades
Priority: remove -1 cards first, then add rolling modifiers
Optimized deck turns average attacks into devastating ones

summary.title: Buy Essential Items Early
bullets: Stamina Potion: recover 2 discards = extra rounds of play
Minor Healing Potion: 5 HP at critical moment
Boots of Striding: +2 movement; don't hoard gold — spend it

walkthrough.title: Loss Card Calculus
walkthrough.text: Advanced players calculate the exact value of a loss card before playing it. Count how many rounds you have left. If playing a loss card shortens your game by one round, that loss card needs to produce more value than an entire round of normal actions. In the first room, that is almost never true. In the final room, even mediocre loss cards are worth playing because the scenario is about to end.

walkthrough.title: Initiative Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Expert players choose initiative based on the monsters' revealed actions, not just their own plans. If the monster ability card shows a strong attack, go fast to kill or stun the monster before it acts. If the monster card shows only movement with no attack, go slow to let allies set up. Late-game initiative manipulation can effectively nullify an entire monster turn.

walkthrough.title: Monster AI Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Monsters follow strict focus rules. They target the enemy they can reach and attack with the least movement. You can exploit this by positioning a tanky character closer while squishier characters stay behind obstacles or at longer range. If a monster cannot find a valid path to attack anyone, it will not move at all. Use this to stall dangerous enemies while you deal with weaker ones.

walkthrough.title: Perk Optimization Path
walkthrough.text: Not all perks are equal. The highest-priority perks remove minus-one cards, shrinking your deck's negative variance. Next, add rolling modifiers that provide extra damage, elements, or conditions without replacing your base draw. Avoid adding non-rolling zero cards early because they dilute your deck. The ideal late-game modifier deck has almost no negatives and stacks rolling bonuses on every attack.

walkthrough.title: Element Chain Planning
walkthrough.text: Coordinating elements across the party unlocks devastating combos. One character generates Fire on turn one, and another consumes it on turn two for a massive bonus effect. Map out which of your ability cards generate and consume elements, then build your hand selection around these chains. In a well-coordinated party, elements flow from one player to the next almost every round.

walkthrough.title: Rest Timing Optimization
walkthrough.text: Knowing when to short rest versus long rest is a game-defining skill. Short rests are free but randomly lose a card. Long rests cost your entire turn but let you choose which card to lose, heal two HP, and refresh items. Long rest when your Stamina Potion is spent and you need to refresh it, or when you need the healing. Short rest when you cannot afford to lose a turn and the randomly lost card is acceptable.

walkthrough.title: Scenario Difficulty Tuning
walkthrough.text: The recommended scenario level is the average party level divided by two, rounded up. But you can adjust one level up or down. Playing one level higher gives more gold and XP per scenario, accelerating your progression. Playing one level lower when a scenario is proving brutal is not a sign of weakness. Completing a scenario at lower difficulty is always better than failing repeatedly at higher difficulty.

walkthrough.title: Enhancement Priorities
walkthrough.text: Enhancements are permanent upgrades you can buy for ability cards between scenarios. They are expensive but compound over many scenarios. The best first enhancement is usually adding a relevant element generation to a non-loss card you use every scenario. Adding plus-one damage to a multi-target attack is also excellent. Avoid enhancing loss cards because you play them less often, reducing the investment's value.

summary.title: Loss Card Calculus
bullets: Loss card must outvalue 1 full round of normal actions
Room 1: almost never worth it; final room: almost always worth it
Count remaining rounds before deciding

summary.title: Initiative Manipulation
bullets: Read monster ability card → decide fast or slow
Monster shows strong attack → go fast, kill/stun before it acts
Monster shows only movement → go slow, let allies set up

summary.title: Monster AI Exploitation
bullets: Monsters focus by least-movement path to attack
Tank closer + squishies behind obstacles → control focus
No valid attack path = monster doesn't move at all

summary.title: Perk Optimization Path
bullets: Priority 1: remove -1 cards (reduce negative variance)
Priority 2: add rolling modifiers (+dmg, elements, conditions)
Avoid non-rolling +0 cards early (deck dilution)
Ideal: no negatives + stacking rolling bonuses

summary.title: Element Chain Planning
bullets: Player A generates element → Player B consumes next turn
Build hand selection around generation/consumption chains
Well-coordinated party flows elements almost every round

summary.title: Rest Timing Optimization
bullets: Short rest: free, random card loss — use when you can't lose a turn
Long rest: costs turn, choose card to lose, heal 2, refresh items
Long rest when Stamina Potion spent or need healing

summary.title: Scenario Difficulty Tuning
bullets: Recommended level = avg party level / 2 (round up), adjust +/- 1
Higher: more gold + XP per scenario
Lower: completing > failing repeatedly; no shame in adjusting

summary.title: Enhancement Priorities
bullets: Best first: add element generation to a non-loss card used every scenario
+1 dmg on multi-target attacks = excellent value
Avoid enhancing loss cards (played less often, lower return)

entries.definition: A card with a top action and a bottom action. Players select two ability cards each round and play the top of one and the bottom of the other. Each class has a unique set of ability cards from level 1 through level 9.
A number printed on each ability card that determines turn order. Lower initiative goes first. Players announce the initiative of one of their two selected cards at the start of each round.
The maximum number of ability cards a class can bring into a scenario. Ranges from 8 (Spellweaver) to 12 (Tinkerer). Hand size determines how many rounds a character can last before exhaustion.

An ability card marked with the X icon. After use, it goes to the lost pile and cannot be recovered for the rest of the scenario. Loss cards typically have powerful effects but permanently reduce your endurance.
The pile where played ability cards go after use (unless they have the loss icon). Cards in the discard pile are recovered when resting.
The pile where loss cards and cards sacrificed to negate damage go. Cards in the lost pile cannot be recovered for the rest of the scenario. The Spellweaver's Reviving Ether is the sole exception.

When a character cannot play two cards from their hand at the start of a round and cannot rest, they are exhausted and removed from the scenario. They keep earned XP and gold but cannot participate further.
Instead of a card's printed action, a player may use Attack 2 for any top half or Move 2 for any bottom half. Default actions are always available as alternatives.
Taken at the end of any round. Recover all discarded cards, then randomly lose one to the lost pile. May suffer 1 damage to re-draw once. Does not cost a turn.
Declared instead of selecting two cards. Initiative is 99. On your turn: recover all discards, choose one to lose, heal 2 HP, refresh all spent items. No ability cards are played.
A 20-card deck drawn from during every attack. Standard composition: 6x +0, 5x +1, 5x -1, 1x +2, 1x -2, 1x Miss (x0), 1x Critical (x2). Modified by perks over time.
An attack modifier card that reduces the attack's damage to zero. When drawn, the modifier deck reshuffles at end of round. Can be removed via the Bless/Curse system or perks.
An attack modifier card that doubles the base attack value. When drawn, the modifier deck reshuffles at end of round. One of the most impactful draws in the game.
A modifier card with a rolling icon. When drawn, draw additional cards until a non-rolling card appears, then sum all rolling values together with the final card. Added to decks via perks.
Draw two attack modifier cards and use the better result. Granted by the Strengthen condition and certain abilities. Significantly increases average attack damage.
Draw two attack modifier cards and use the worse result. Caused by the Muddle condition, ranged attacks while adjacent to enemies, and certain abilities.
Reduces each incoming attack's damage by the listed value. Applied after the attack modifier card. Multiple Shield effects stack. Typically lasts until end of next turn.
When attacked by an enemy within the specified range, deal the listed damage back automatically. Does not use attack modifier cards. Melee retaliate requires adjacent attacker.
Ignore a specified amount of the target's Shield value when attacking. Pierce 3 against Shield 2 means the full Shield is negated.
When an attack lists Target X, the attacker hits X separate enemies within range, drawing a separate modifier card for each. Multi-target attacks are among the most powerful in the game.
Condition. All sources of damage against the poisoned figure deal +1 damage. Removed by any Heal effect, which consumes the heal without restoring HP.
Condition. The affected figure suffers 1 damage at the start of each of its turns. Removed by any Heal effect, which consumes the heal without restoring HP.
Condition. The affected figure loses its entire next turn — no actions at all. Removed at the end of the lost turn. The most powerful single condition in the game.
Condition. The affected figure cannot perform any Move abilities on its next turn. Removed at end of the affected turn. Does not prevent attacks or other actions.
Condition. The affected figure cannot perform any Attack abilities on its next turn. Removed at end of the affected turn. Does not prevent movement or other actions.
Condition. The affected figure has Disadvantage on all attacks on its next turn. Removed at end of the affected turn. Very useful for reducing incoming damage from strong enemies.
Condition. The affected figure has Advantage on all attacks on its next turn. Removed at end of the affected turn. Apply before big attacks to maximize modifier draw quality.
Condition. The affected figure cannot be focused or targeted by enemies. Removed at end of the figure's next turn. Does not prevent area-of-effect damage or Retaliate.
Adds a x0 (Miss) card to the target's attack modifier deck. Removed when the Curse card is drawn. Effectively increases the chance of the target missing an attack.
Adds a x2 (Critical) card to the target's attack modifier deck. Removed when the Bless card is drawn. Powerful buff that increases the chance of a double-damage attack.
One of six elements. Generated and consumed by various ability cards. Often associated with damage-boosting effects. Follows the Strong → Waning → Inert cycle.
One of six elements. Often associated with healing, Shield, and crowd-control enhancements. Generated primarily by the Spellweaver and Cryomancer-themed classes.
One of six elements. Often associated with movement bonuses and ranged attack enhancements. Used by several classes for mobility-related consumption effects.
One of six elements. Often associated with defensive bonuses and healing enhancements. The Cragheart frequently generates and consumes Earth.
One of six elements. Often associated with healing, Strengthen, and holy-themed effects. Several support-oriented classes generate Light.
One of six elements. Often associated with Poison, Curse, and stealth-themed effects. The Mindthief and Nightshroud frequently use Dark.
Tracks the state of all six elements: Strong (just generated), Waning (generated last round), and Inert (unavailable). At end of each round, Strong moves to Waning and Waning moves to Inert.
A map overlay that blocks movement and line of sight. Characters and monsters cannot enter obstacle hexes unless an ability specifically allows it (e.g., Jump or Flying).
A map overlay that triggers when any figure enters its hex. The figure suffers the listed damage and/or condition, then the trap token is removed from the map.
A map overlay that costs 2 movement points to enter instead of 1. Does not affect flying or jumping figures. Slows movement but does not deal damage.
A map overlay that deals damage to any figure that enters it. Unlike traps, hazardous terrain is not removed after triggering — it persists and damages every time it is entered.
An overlay between rooms. When any figure enters a closed door hex, the door opens, the active turn pauses, and the new room is revealed and set up. The figure then resumes their turn.
A movement modifier that ignores all figures, obstacles, traps, and terrain between the starting and ending hexes. Only the destination hex matters for triggering effects.
A movement type that ignores all terrain and figure restrictions. Flying figures can end movement on obstacles or traps without triggering them. Very rare and powerful.
Required for ranged attacks. Draw an imaginary line from any corner of your hex to any corner of the target hex. If the line does not pass through a wall or obstacle hex, you have line of sight.
A numbered overlay tile placed during scenario setup. When looted, the player checks the treasure index in the scenario book for the reward. Often contains unique items or bonus gold.
Determines monster stats, trap damage, gold per coin, and bonus XP. Calculated as average party level divided by 2, rounded up. May be adjusted +1 or -1 by player choice.

Gloomhaven's city prosperity level, ranging from 1 to 9. Increases through retirements and certain events. Higher prosperity unlocks better shop items and allows new characters to start at higher levels.

Party reputation ranges from -20 to +20 and affects shop prices and event outcomes. Positive reputation grants discounts; negative reputation increases prices. Changed by scenario outcomes and event choices.
A secret card drawn at character creation that defines the character's retirement condition. Examples include killing a certain number of specific enemies or completing specific scenarios.
When a character fulfills their personal quest, they must retire. This unlocks a new sealed character class, increases prosperity, and the player creates a new character.
A permanent campaign marker earned by completing certain scenarios. Global achievements open or close future scenario options and affect event outcomes for all parties.
An achievement specific to your adventuring party. Tracks progress and decisions unique to your group, separate from global achievements that affect all parties.

A permanent upgrade purchased between scenarios that adds an effect to one ability card. Examples include +1 damage, element generation, or adding a condition. Enhancements persist through retirement.
A permanent modification to your attack modifier deck earned from battle goals and leveling up. Each class has a unique perk list. Perks remove negative cards or add powerful new ones.

A secret objective drawn at the start of each scenario. Completing it earns a perk checkmark. Examples include not using loss cards, looting a certain number of coins, or killing a specific number of enemies.
A narrative card drawn between scenarios while in Gloomhaven. Read the front, choose option A or B, flip for consequences. May affect gold, reputation, prosperity, or unlock content.
A narrative card drawn when traveling to a scenario not linked from the previous location. Resolved identically to city events. May affect HP, gold, conditions, or reputation before the scenario.
The rule determining which enemy a monster targets. Monsters focus on the enemy they can reach and attack with the least movement. Ties broken by lowest initiative value.

A stronger version of a monster type, indicated by a gold/yellow base. Elites have higher HP, attack, and often additional abilities compared to normal monsters. Elites act before normals of the same type.
Drawn each round for each active monster type. Determines the monster's initiative, movement, attack, and any special actions for that round. All monsters of the same type use the same card.
A figure placed by an ability card in an adjacent empty hex. Summons have their own HP and stats, act on the summoner's initiative, and follow a basic AI. They are not controlled directly.
Force a target figure a specified number of hexes away from you. The target cannot be pushed through walls or obstacles. Push can move enemies into traps or hazardous terrain.
Force a target figure a specified number of hexes toward you. The target cannot be pulled through walls or obstacles. Pull can move enemies into traps or hazardous terrain.
Earned during scenarios by performing actions marked with XP icons and by completing the scenario. Accumulated XP determines when a character levels up between scenarios.
The game's currency, earned by looting money tokens during scenarios and from scenario completion rewards. Spent on items, enhancements, and donations between scenarios.
Equipment cards purchased from the Gloomhaven shop. Items have slot types (head, body, legs, one-hand, two-hand, small) and provide passive bonuses, activated effects, or consumable one-time uses.
An action that picks up all money tokens and treasure tiles within the specified range. Loot 1 covers your hex and all adjacent hexes. The primary way to earn gold during scenarios.
An action that restores hit points to a target up to their maximum HP. If the target has Poison or Wound, the Heal removes that condition first and is consumed without restoring any HP.
